sourceMovie.title: Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth for the same reason it broke through in 1997: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a drama rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Hideaki Anno keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The animation and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth fans keep landing on Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion (1997) — the animation is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone draws from the same animation well as Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth, channeling drama in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2007 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance (2009) captures the animation appeal of Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth without simply imitating it. The drama streak running through both films is what puts Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Akira
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The animation appeal of Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth — that uplifting edge, the science-fiction — translates cleanly into Akira (1988). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Final Fantasy VII: Advent Children
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Final Fantasy VII: Advent Children (2005) clearly understood what makes animation work — the same quality that keeps Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth rewatchable. Expect the same uplifting rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Spirited Away
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Spirited Away (2001) is the kind of film Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The animation overlap is real, but it's the uplifting emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Donnie Darko
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Donnie Darko shares Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth's gift for drama — the 2001 film delivers fantasy without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Requiem for a Dream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth compelling — the drama, the crime — shows up in Requiem for a Dream (2000) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Trainspotting
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama energy of Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth is alive in Trainspotting (1996), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Apocalypse Now
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Apocalypse Now (1979) operates in the same drama as Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The tense payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

faq.question: What should I watch after Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth?
faq.answer: Try Neon Genesis Evangelion: The End of Evangelion, Evangelion: 1.0 You Are (Not) Alone, Evangelion: 2.0 You Can (Not) Advance—each shares DNA with Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth?
faq.answer: Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth (1997) is best described as Animation, Drama, Science Fiction. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Neon Genesis Evangelion: Death and Rebirth part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Neon Genesis Evangelion Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
